title: RTPWebb
content:
Student Required Technology Practices 
 
 
The network and Internet are great resources for the Webb campus. As is the case with most privileges, there are 
inherent responsibilities. Every member of the community is responsible for maintaining their networked 
devices. If every member of the community works to keep their devices maintained, and acts responsibly, the 
network will be a safe, reliable, and useful resource. 
 
There are three simple requirements if you are connecting devices to the school network: 
1. Install anti-virus and anti-malware software on your system. 
2. K eep your system and software current with the latest security patches and updates. 
3. Register your devices on the school network. 
 
Not knowing how to maintain your devices affects you as an individual and the entire Webb community. If a device 
is not maintained properly, or network bandwidth is abused, the offending device will be removed from the 
network. Ignorance is not an excuse. Ongoing violations can result in permanent loss of network privileges. The 
tech staff will do what they can to assist you in maintaining your devices. However, it is the responsibility of the 
student to maintain their computer and other digital devices. 
 
It is important for students to use common sense and be conscientious of what is said in all communications. 
Often times an email, text, or social media post can be misinterpreted. Any form of harassment, intentional 
or not, that is illegal will not be tolerated no matter if it was done in person or through the use of technology. 
 
Internet  
Wireless connectivity and data ports are provided throughout the entire campus for faculty, staff, and students to 
access the Internet. The school Internet connection has a content filter and firewall to protect the campus from 
security attacks. The Internet is provided to enhance the academic program, as well as provide personal benefits 
like staying connected with friends and family. However, personal activities that slow down or interfere with 
academic activities will not be permitted. There are a few guidelines that each individual of the community must 
abide by: 
 
1.   Peer to Peer, proxies, and bit torrent programs are not allowed. 
2.   The use of switches and wireless routers is prohibited. 
3.   A device that is connected through a hot spot to the Internet should not be plugged into the school network. 
4.   Students may not share their personal hot spots with other students. 
5.   Students must abide by all school policies and rules whether using a hot spot or through the school 
connection. 
6.   Any material that could be classified as offensive or illegal should not be downloaded or distributed. 
 
File Storage  
The school provides each student with an Office 365 account with a 1 Terabyte One Drive. This gives students 
access to their files from any device connected to the Internet with a browser. However, the school takes no 
responsibility in maintaining these files. If they are deleted they cannot be recovered. The tech department 
does not maintain a backup of these files. 
 
There is no expectation to privacy for files or data that students upload, download, or transfer across the 
network. Students are responsible for maintaining their privacy and using common sense in where and how they 
transfer information. Email, Office 365, social media, and network folders are not places to keep personal 
information. 
 
Viruses, Spyware, Ransomware & Malware  
Systems are infected with viruses and malware on a regular basis sometimes without ever giving any signs that 
the system has been inf ected. The virus es of today come in two varieties, there are the extremely destructive 
viruses like ransomware and then there are those that hide on your system collecting data.  That is why it is 
essential that all systems have anti-virus software loaded on them. It is important you follow these steps:

1. Install anti-virus and anti-malware software on your system and keep it updated.
2. If you fail to install and keep updated virus scanning software on your system, you will be disconnected from the 
school network.
3. If you discover a virus on your system, it needs to be removed immediately.
4. If you require the assistance of the tech team in removing the virus, you need to bring your machine to the 
Fawcett Library technology office.
5. To minimize the outbreaks of viruses, read the postings in Webb News on First Class.
6. If your machine is detected by the technology team as having been infected, the machine will be disconnected 
from the network until the technology team verifies that the machine is clean.
7. Do not open attachments or click links in emails if you do not 100% trust and know that it is not a virus.
8. Use common sense
Copyright  
In the digital age it is quite simple to get access to and reuse information. With this ability comes the responsibility 
of giving credit to the owner/creator of the information. Students are allowed to use information from the Internet 
for schoolwork as long as they give the appropriate credit, and it is only used for academic purposes. 
1. Students are not allowed to use text, photos, music, or any other medias from the Internet or other digital
sources for projects that would be used outside of the classroom or for profit without getting the appropriate
permission from the owner.
2. F
or more information about copyright laws check the following web sites: 
a.  http://fairuse.stanford.edu
b.  http://lcweb.loc.gov/copyright/ 
June 2023
*This document is updated regularly and should be reviewed annually.
